audit_status.source_base: Hebrew Judges / Shoftim anti-Benjamin oath, Jabesh-gilead massacre, captive women, Shiloh abductions, tribal restoration, and closing refrain narrative, draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for Mizpah marriage oath, lament over tribal loss, death oath against assembly absentees, targeting Jabesh-gilead, killing men, partnered women, and children, seizure of four hundred young women, peace offer to Rimmon survivors, insufficiency of captives, elders' legal evasion, Shiloh festival geography, vineyard ambush, abduction and forced marriage, family complaint management, Benjaminite rebuilding, and final no-king/right-in-own-eyes refrain.

verses.restored: Men of Israel had sworn at Mizpah, saying, No man from us shall give his daughter to Benjamin as wife.
The people came to Bethel and sat there until evening before Divine. They lifted their voice and wept a great weeping.
They said, Why, YHWH, Divine of Israel, has this happened in Israel, that one tribe should be missing today from Israel?
On next day the people arose early, built there an altar, and offered ascending offerings and peace offerings.

Children of Israel said, Who is there from all tribes of Israel who did not come up in assembly to YHWH? For a great oath had been made concerning one who did not come up to YHWH at Mizpah, saying, Being put to death, he shall be put to death.
Children of Israel regretted concerning Benjamin their brother and said, One tribe has been cut off today from Israel.
What shall we do for wives for those who remain, since we ourselves have sworn by YHWH not to give them from our daughters as wives?
They said, Who is one from tribes of Israel who did not come up to YHWH at Mizpah? Look, no man had come to camp from Jabesh-gilead to assembly.
The people were counted, and look, there was no man there from inhabitants of Jabesh-gilead.
The congregation sent there twelve thousand men from sons of strength and commanded them, saying, Go and strike inhabitants of Jabesh-gilead by edge of sword, including women and little ones.
This is the thing that you shall do: every male and every woman who has known lying with a male you shall devote to destruction.
They found among inhabitants of Jabesh-gilead four hundred young women who had not known a man by lying with a male. They brought them to camp at Shiloh, which is in land of Canaan.
All congregation sent and spoke to children of Benjamin who were at rock of Rimmon and proclaimed peace to them.
Benjamin returned at that time, and they gave them the women whom they had kept alive from women of Jabesh-gilead, but they did not find enough for them.
The people regretted concerning Benjamin, because YHWH had made a breach among tribes of Israel.
Elders of congregation said, What shall we do for wives for those who remain, since women have been destroyed from Benjamin?
They said, There must be an inheritance for survivors of Benjamin, so a tribe may not be wiped out from Israel.
But we are not able to give them wives from our daughters, because children of Israel had sworn, saying, Cursed is one who gives a wife to Benjamin.
They said, Look, there is a feast of YHWH from days to days at Shiloh, which is north of Bethel, east of highway that goes up from Bethel to Shechem, and south of Lebonah.
They commanded children of Benjamin, saying, Go and lie in wait in vineyards.
Watch, and look: if daughters of Shiloh come out to dance in dances, then come out from vineyards and seize for yourselves, each man his wife, from daughters of Shiloh, and go to land of Benjamin.

When their fathers or their brothers come to contend with us, we will say to them, Show them favor for our sake, because we did not take for each man his wife in battle, nor did you give them to them at this time so as to become guilty.
Children of Benjamin did so. They carried away wives according to their number from dancing women whom they seized. They went and returned to their inheritance, built cities, and dwelt in them.
Children of Israel departed from there at that time, each man to his tribe and to his clan; they went out from there, each man to his inheritance.
In those days there was no king in Israel; each one did what was right in one's own eyes.

translation_notes: The oath not to give daughters to Benjamin is a human wartime vow. Like Jephthah's vow, it does not become righteous merely because YHWH's name is invoked. Harmful vows should be renounced, repaired, or lawfully released—not fulfilled through further victims.

Israel laments the possible loss of Benjamin but does not first lament Benjaminite civilians, Jabesh-gilead, the murdered concubine, or the women whose lives will be assigned as a solution. Grief for institutional continuity can obscure grief for persons.

A second oath threatens death to communities absent from Mizpah. The assembly uses one reckless oath to solve another, demonstrating how absolutist group promises multiply coercion rather than create faithfulness.
Jabesh-gilead's nonattendance does not justify massacre. Verse 10 explicitly includes women and little ones, making this collective punishment and mass killing of civilians.

The herem/devotion command in verse 11 is issued by the congregation in service of its plan. It must not be universalized as Divine permission for genocide, forced purity, or killing based on sex, relationship history, or community membership.

The four hundred young women are selected by male-defined sexual status after their families and community are killed. Their survival is captivity, not rescue; their lack of prior intercourse is not consent, availability, purity superiority, or a definition of womanhood.

Peace is proclaimed to the six hundred Benjaminite men while captive women are transferred to them. Reconciliation among armed men is purchased with women's autonomy and cannot be called restorative justice.
The elders treat women's bodies as a numerical resource for preserving male lineage. Tribal continuity does not override bodily autonomy, grief, kinship, or the right not to marry.
The Shiloh festival and dancing do not imply sexual or marital consent. Public celebration, clothing, movement, joy, youth, or presence outdoors never authorizes seizure.

The vineyard plan is organized abduction and forced marriage. The euphemism of 'take wives' must not conceal kidnapping, coercive confinement, likely sexual violence, and permanent disruption of the women's lives.
Verse 22 exploits a legal technicality: families did not voluntarily 'give' daughters, so the oath can be claimed intact. Technical compliance that engineers foreseeable abuse is not moral innocence.

Benjamin's rebuilding is narrated after women are seized, but restored cities do not repair murdered communities or coerced marriages. Survival of a tribe cannot be celebrated apart from the costs imposed on others.

The final refrain diagnoses a society where each acts according to private judgment. It does not prove that monarchy, authoritarianism, patriarchy, or a stronger coercive state would automatically create justice.
safety_note: Maximum anti-abduction, anti-forced-marriage, anti-genocide, and anti-women-as-property safeguards: Do not present Jabesh-gilead's massacre as obedience, the four hundred captives as saved brides, or the Shiloh abductions as romance. Oaths, tribal survival, lineage, festivals, family negotiations, and legal technicalities cannot override consent. Do not use sexual history to rank women, define gender, shame survivors, or mark anyone as available. The book ends with institutions preserving themselves by transferring violence onto women; that is the indictment, not the solution.
